seo.title: Building AI Code Generation with open-source tools
seo.description: A step-by-step implementation guide for AI Code Generation with open-source tools.

content.intro: This guide outlines a professional-grade workflow for integrating AI code generation into production environments. It focuses on minimizing hallucinations, ensuring architectural consistency, and automating the verification of AI-produced code through structured context and iterative testing.
prerequisites: Access to an AI coding assistant (Cursor, GitHub Copilot, or Aider)
Existing CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, or similar)
Established unit testing suite in the target repository
Familiarity with Markdown for context documentation

steps.title: Define Project-Specific Context and Rules
steps.explanation: Create a configuration file (e.g., .cursorrules or a global system prompt) that defines your tech stack versions, naming conventions, and architectural constraints. This prevents the AI from suggesting deprecated libraries or patterns that deviate from your codebase.

code.content:
# .cursorrules
- Use TypeScript for all new files.
- Prefer functional components over classes.
- Use Tailwind CSS for styling; do not use CSS modules.
- All data fetching must use the custom `useApi` hook.
- Ensure every generated function has a JSDoc block with @param and @returns.

pitfalls: Overloading the rules file with too many instructions, which can lead to the AI ignoring specific constraints.
Failing to update the rules when the tech stack or conventions evolve.

steps.title: Implement a Design-First Generation Loop
steps.explanation: Before generating implementation code, prompt the AI to generate a technical specification or 'pseudocode plan'. Review this plan to ensure the logic handles edge cases and integrates correctly with existing services. Use a tool like Claude Code or Aider to iterate on the plan before writing a single line of application code.
pitfalls: Accepting an implementation immediately without verifying the underlying logic first.
Allowing the AI to create new abstractions where existing ones should be reused.

steps.title: Automate Test-Driven Code Refinement
steps.explanation: Generate unit tests simultaneously with the implementation. Use a CLI-based assistant to run the test suite and feed the error output back into the AI to fix bugs automatically. This creates a closed-loop system where the AI self-corrects based on runtime feedback.

code.content:
# Example workflow using Aider CLI
aider --message "Create a utility to parse JWTs" --test "npm test src/utils/jwt.test.ts"

pitfalls: The AI may 'fix' tests to pass by weakening assertions rather than fixing the code.
Circular logic where the AI generates both the bug and the incorrect test to validate it.

steps.title: Configure Security and Dependency Gating
steps.explanation: AI models often suggest outdated or non-existent packages (hallucinations). Integrate a dependency scanner like Snyk or Socket into your CI/CD pipeline to flag vulnerable or suspicious packages introduced by AI-generated PRs. Set up a pre-commit hook to run 'npm audit' or equivalent.

code.content:
name: Security Scan
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  snyk:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Run Snyk to check for vulnerabilities
        uses: snyk/actions/node@master
        env:
          SNYK_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SNYK_TOKEN }}

pitfalls: Blindly running 'npm install' on AI-suggested commands without verifying package authenticity.
Ignoring security warnings in the rush to merge AI-generated features.

steps.title: Establish an AI-Specific Code Review Protocol
steps.explanation: Human reviewers must treat AI-generated code with higher scrutiny than human-written code. Use a checklist that specifically targets common AI errors: hallucinated API parameters, lack of proper error handling in async blocks, and inefficient loops that could lead to performance bottlenecks.
pitfalls: Reviewer fatigue leading to 'rubber-stamping' large AI-generated diffs.
Assuming that code which passes tests is architecturally sound or maintainable.

steps.title: Monitor and Version AI Prompts as Assets
steps.explanation: Treat complex prompts used for generating boilerplate or migrations as code assets. Store them in a `/prompts` directory in your repository. This allows the team to version-control the 'instructions' that produce specific outputs, ensuring reproducibility across different developer machines.

code.content:
PROMPT_NAME: Database Migration Generator
CONTEXT: Prisma, PostgreSQL
INSTRUCTIONS: Generate a migration that adds a 'deleted_at' column to the provided table list, ensuring all existing queries are updated to filter out null values.

pitfalls: Losing successful prompts in individual developer chat histories.
Inconsistent output when different team members use slightly different versions of the same prompt.

content.conclusion: By moving from ad-hoc chatting to a structured, test-driven, and context-aware workflow, teams can leverage AI code generation while maintaining production standards. The key is to treat AI as a junior developer who requires precise constraints, automated verification, and rigorous human oversight.
